topic: Two friends plan separate birthday parties on the same day
raw_output:
[story 1]

The calendar page for the 28th was mocking me. Liam’s grin, the way he’d excitedly detailed his plans for a low-key barbecue, still echoed in my mind. I stared at the invitation for Sarah’s gathering, the vibrant colours seeming too loud, too celebratory for the knot tightening in my chest. Every time I looked at the date, a sharp, unpleasant clench tightened around my stomach. I spent the entire morning meticulously arranging my own small collection of decorations, the careful placement of streamers feeling like a desperate attempt to balance a scale that was hopelessly tipped. When Liam called to confirm the time for his event, my voice came out unnaturally high, a thin veneer over the churning anxiety inside. "Yeah, sounds great," I managed, forcing a smile that felt brittle on my face. The thought of him having such a perfect day, surrounded by people who genuinely loved him, made my own solitary preparations feel like a betrayal.

[story 2]

I kept catching myself glancing at my phone, refreshing Liam’s messaging thread, a reflexive habit born of sheer, overwhelming unease. The air in my apartment felt thick, suffocating. I had meticulously planned my solo celebration—a quiet evening with a good book and takeout—but the meticulousness was a shield. It was a way to manage the internal pressure building behind my eyes. Seeing the pictures of Liam’s planned festivities, the laughter radiating from the photos, sent a cold tremor through my hands. I walked over to the window, watching the streetlights flicker on, feeling a profound sense of wrongness settle deep in my bones. I needed to do *something* to counteract the imbalance, to somehow make the day feel less skewed, even though the intention was purely selfish.

[story 3]

"Are you sure you don't want to come?" Liam asked, his tone light, completely oblivious to the internal storm raging within me. I shook my head, the movement jerky. "No, I think I need some space today. I have something else planned." The lie tasted like ash in my mouth. I watched him nod, his easy confidence a stark contrast to the heavy weight I carried. Back in my room, I started reorganizing my bookshelf, pushing the novels into perfect, symmetrical rows. Each book spine felt like a small, rigid piece of armor. The silence of the room amplified the sound of my own shallow breathing. I felt a deep, undeniable pull towards joining him, to sharing the joy, but a stronger, more insistent force kept me rooted to my own quiet corner, a self-imposed exile.
